meanings.definition: An instrument for measuring or testing something.
examples: The pressure gauge showed the tank was nearly full.
He checked the fuel gauge before the long drive.

meanings.definition: Using more words than necessary; wordy.
examples: The report was so verbose that few people finished reading it.
He gave a verbose explanation when a single sentence would have done.

meanings.definition: Having many mountains; full of mountains.
examples: The train wound slowly through the mountainous region.
Switzerland is famous for its mountainous scenery.

meanings.definition: Huge in size, like a mountain.
examples: Mountainous waves crashed over the deck of the ship.

meanings.definition: To make something old-fashioned or out of date.
examples: New technology quickly antiquated the bulky old machines.
Modern methods have antiquated many of the craftsman's tools.

meanings.definition: A high, prominent point of land jutting out into the sea.
examples: A lighthouse stood on the rocky promontory.
From the promontory, they watched the waves break far below.

meanings.definition: To vary the tone, pitch, or volume of one's voice or a sound.
examples: A good speaker knows how to modulate her voice for effect.
He modulated his tone to soothe the frightened child.

meanings.definition: To adjust or regulate something to a proper level or degree.
examples: The thermostat modulates the temperature throughout the day.

meanings.definition: Lasting for only a short time; quickly fading.
examples: The evanescent beauty of the sunset vanished within minutes.
Fame can be an evanescent thing, here today and gone tomorrow.

meanings.definition: A statement expressing regret and disclaiming intentional error or offense.
examples: He offered a sincere apology for arriving late.
She accepted his apology and the matter was forgotten.

meanings.definition: A formal defense or justification of a belief or position.
examples: The essay was an apology for the principles of free trade.

meanings.definition: Filled with or obscured by fog.
examples: The foggy morning made driving slow and dangerous.
Ships sounded their horns in the foggy harbor.

meanings.definition: Unclear or confused; obscure.
examples: My memory of that distant night is foggy at best.

meanings.definition: To say in advance that something will happen; to foretell.
examples: Forecasters predict heavy rain for the weekend.
No one could have predicted such an extraordinary outcome.

meanings.definition: The quality of being extremely poisonous, harmful, or severe.
examples: The virulence of the venom meant that even a small bite could be fatal.
Scientists measured the virulence of the new strain of the virus.

meanings.definition: Bitter hostility or harshness of feeling, language, or tone.
examples: The virulence of his criticism shocked everyone in the room.

meanings.definition: To combine or unite separate things into one unified whole.
examples: The two small banks agreed to amalgamate into a single institution.
Several local clubs amalgamated to form one national organization.

meanings.definition: Absolute or unrestricted power held by a single ruler.
examples: Under the new regime, the dictator exercised a near-total autarchy.
The constitution was written to prevent any one leader from claiming autarchy.

meanings.definition: An officer of the law whose duty is to keep the peace.
examples: The village constable was the first to arrive at the scene of the disturbance.
She reported the theft to the local constable.

meanings.definition: Inclined to plunder, exploit, or prey on others for gain.
examples: The company was accused of predatory lending practices that trapped borrowers in debt.
Predatory raiders swept down on the undefended coastal towns.

meanings.definition: Living by capturing and feeding on other animals.
examples: Hawks and other predatory birds circled high above the field.

meanings.definition: A dishonest or unprincipled person; a villain.
examples: The scoundrel cheated dozens of investors out of their savings.
She called him a scoundrel for abandoning his promises.

meanings.definition: Extremely cold, like ice.
examples: A glacial wind cut through the mountain pass.
The water in the stream was glacial even in summer.

meanings.definition: Very slow, like the movement of a glacier.
examples: Progress on the project proceeded at a glacial pace.

meanings.definition: Cold and unfriendly in manner.
examples: She gave him a glacial stare and left the room.

meanings.definition: A laborer who is hired and paid by the day.
examples: As a day-man, he never knew whether there would be work tomorrow.
The farmer hired several day-men to bring in the harvest.

meanings.definition: Sincere and innocent, without cunning or pretense.
examples: Her artless honesty made everyone trust her at once.
He answered the question with the artless candor of a child.

meanings.definition: Not allowed or permitted.
examples: Using a phone during the exam is strictly impermissible.
The judge ruled the evidence impermissible and excluded it from the trial.

meanings.definition: A stage entertainment made up of a series of varied acts such as songs, comedy, and dance.
examples: Her grandparents had performed in vaudeville before the rise of film.
The old theater once hosted nightly vaudeville shows.

meanings.definition: Tending to stimulate thought, reflection, or further ideas.
examples: The painting was suggestive of a stormy sea at dusk.
Her opening remarks were suggestive and gave the audience much to ponder.

meanings.definition: Hinting at something improper or indecent.
examples: The comedian's suggestive jokes made some guests uncomfortable.

meanings.definition: The quality of being powerful, effective, or influential.
examples: The potency of the medicine fades once the bottle is opened.
No one doubted the potency of her argument.

meanings.definition: To take part in or share in an activity or event.
examples: All students are encouraged to participate in the class discussion.
She chose not to participate in the protest.

meanings.definition: An unpleasant or harmful smell or vapor given off by decaying matter.
examples: A foul effluvium rose from the stagnant marsh.
The effluvium of the old factory lingered over the neighborhood.

meanings.definition: The process of coming into view or becoming known.
examples: The emergence of the sun from behind the clouds lifted everyone's mood.
We are witnessing the emergence of a new political movement.

meanings.definition: The act of beginning something again after a pause or interruption.
examples: The resumption of talks gave both sides reason for hope.
After the storm, the resumption of flights took several hours.

meanings.definition: To move or guide skillfully, especially through a difficult course.
examples: She maneuvered the car carefully into the narrow parking space.
He maneuvered the boat between the rocks.

meanings.definition: To manage a situation cleverly using strategy or scheming.
examples: He maneuvered his way into the chairman's good graces.

meanings.definition: Able to be forgiven or justified; pardonable.
examples: A small mistake under such pressure is entirely excusable.
His lateness was excusable given the traffic accident.

meanings.definition: To go along with someone or something as a companion.
examples: She offered to accompany him to the doctor's office.
Thunder usually accompanies a flash of lightning.

meanings.definition: To play a musical part that supports a soloist or singer.
examples: A pianist accompanied the young violinist during the recital.

meanings.definition: To become less intense, severe, or active; to die down.
examples: We waited inside until the storm began to subside.
Her anger gradually subsided as he explained himself.

meanings.definition: To sink to a lower level or settle.
examples: The floodwaters slowly subsided over the next few days.

meanings.definition: To refuse to allow or accept something as valid.
examples: The referee disallowed the goal for an offside infraction.
The court may disallow any claim filed after the deadline.

meanings.definition: Not respectable in character, behavior, or appearance.
examples: He fell in with a disreputable crowd at the docks.
The hotel had a disreputable reputation among travelers.

meanings.definition: A way out, or the act of going out.
examples: The fire marshal checked that every room had a clear egress.
The crowd found egress blocked by a locked gate.

meanings.definition: To behave badly or improperly.
examples: The children began to misbehave the moment the teacher left.
If you misbehave again, you will lose your privileges.

meanings.definition: Of unknown name, source, or authorship.
examples: The poem was published by an anonymous author.
She received an anonymous letter warning her of the danger.

meanings.definition: To give life or energy to; to enliven.
examples: A few bright cushions vivified the dull gray room.
Spring rains vivify the parched fields.

meanings.definition: An account of an event as shaped by one person's perspective or opinion.
examples: Each witness gave a slightly different version of the accident.
His version of the story left out several key details.

meanings.definition: A particular form or variant of something that differs from others.
examples: This is the latest version of the software.

meanings.definition: A person who compiles or writes dictionaries.
examples: As a lexicographer, she spent years documenting how new words were used.
The lexicographer added several hundred entries to the new edition.

meanings.definition: Exceeding what is needed; superfluous or repetitive.
examples: The editor cut several redundant phrases from the essay.
Saying 'free gift' is redundant, since a gift is already free.

meanings.definition: No longer needed for a job and therefore dismissed from employment.
examples: Hundreds of workers were made redundant when the plant closed.

meanings.definition: Reckless and malicious mischief.
examples: The boys were up to all sorts of deviltry behind the barn.
There was a glint of deviltry in her eyes as she planned the prank.

meanings.definition: A lowering of the spirits; a state of sadness or dejection.
examples: A deep depression settled over him after the loss of his job.
She struggled with depression for much of that long winter.

meanings.definition: A sunken or hollow place in a surface.
examples: Rainwater collected in a shallow depression in the road.

meanings.definition: A long period of severe economic decline.
examples: Many businesses failed during the Great Depression.

meanings.definition: A mild or indirect word or phrase substituted for one considered harsh or offensive.
examples: 'Passed away' is a common euphemism for 'died'.
He used polite euphemisms to avoid mentioning the layoffs directly.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that starts a fire, literally or figuratively.
examples: The arsonist was a known incendiary with a record of setting blazes.
His speech was an incendiary that ignited the crowd's fury.

meanings.definition: Tending to stir up conflict or strong feeling.
examples: The newspaper printed an incendiary editorial that outraged readers.

meanings.definition: The act of saying farewell, or the words used in saying it.
examples: He raised his glass in a heartfelt valediction to his old friends.
Her letter closed with a simple valediction.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to the city of Paris.
examples: She admired the elegance of Parisian fashion.
They dined at a small Parisian café near the river.

meanings.definition: A part or element that makes up a larger whole.
examples: Each component of the engine was inspected before assembly.
Trust is an essential component of any friendship.

meanings.definition: To examine something closely and critically in order to understand it.
examples: The scientists analyzed the water samples for contamination.
She analyzed the poem line by line for the class.

meanings.definition: To reduce, limit, or cut short.
examples: The company had to curtail spending during the downturn.
Bad weather curtailed our vacation by two days.

meanings.definition: Full of twists, turns, and bends.
examples: The tortuous mountain road wound back and forth for miles.
We followed a tortuous path through the dense forest.

meanings.definition: Excessively complicated, indirect, or devious.
examples: His explanation was so tortuous that no one could follow it.

meanings.definition: The person to whom money is to be paid.
examples: Write the payee's name clearly on the check.
The bank confirmed the identity of the payee before releasing the funds.

meanings.definition: A person who breaks a law, rule, or agreement.
examples: Repeat violators of the parking rules will be fined.
The treaty set penalties for any violator of its terms.

meanings.definition: Not conforming to accepted standards of right conduct; wicked.
examples: He considered it immoral to profit from another's misfortune.
The novel was once banned as immoral and corrupting.

meanings.definition: Stubborn refusal to change one's opinion or course of action.
examples: His obstinacy made any compromise impossible.
She clung to her plan with unshakable obstinacy.

meanings.definition: Suitable or safe to be eaten; edible.
examples: Not every mushroom in the forest is comestible.
The survival guide listed which wild plants are comestible.

meanings.definition: Taking effect from a date in the past; applying to earlier events.
examples: The pay raise was retroactive to the start of the year.
The new law is not retroactive and applies only to future cases.

meanings.definition: A single-celled microorganism, many kinds of which can cause disease.
examples: A single bacterium can multiply into millions within hours.
Scientists identified the bacterium responsible for the infection.

meanings.definition: To cause severe physical or mental pain to.
examples: The burns excruciated him for weeks.
Guilt continued to excruciate her long after the incident.

meanings.definition: Permitted by law; lawful.
examples: The shop sold only licit goods that met every regulation.
They distinguished carefully between licit and illicit trade.

meanings.definition: Causing horror or disgust; gruesome.
examples: Detectives discovered the grisly remains in the basement.
The accident left behind a grisly scene.

meanings.definition: To restore the brightness or appearance of something; to polish up.
examples: He furbished the old brass lamp until it gleamed.
The team furbished the neglected garden before the open house.

meanings.definition: An introductory statement at the beginning of a book addressing the reader.
examples: In the preface, the author explained why she wrote the book.
I always read the preface before starting the first chapter.

meanings.definition: Following the exact words or basic meaning of the original.
examples: The translation was a literal rendering of the French text.
Taken in its literal sense, the rule makes no allowance for emergencies.

meanings.definition: True to fact and without exaggeration.
examples: It was a literal miracle that everyone survived the crash.

meanings.definition: An involuntary shaking or trembling movement.
examples: A tremor ran through her hands as she opened the letter.
The patient had a slight tremor in his right arm.

meanings.definition: A small earthquake or shaking of the ground.
examples: A minor tremor rattled windows across the city.

meanings.definition: Having enough money to pay all one's debts.
examples: After years of struggle, the business was finally solvent.
The bank assured customers that it remained fully solvent.

meanings.definition: A substance, usually a liquid, that dissolves another substance.
examples: Water is a powerful solvent for many salts.

meanings.definition: Exceeding the usual or required number; extra.
examples: A few supernumerary chairs were placed along the back wall.
The patient was born with a supernumerary finger.

meanings.definition: An extra performer who appears on stage but has no speaking part.
examples: She started her acting career as a supernumerary in the opera.

meanings.definition: A large stringed instrument, the cello, held between the player's knees and played with a bow.
examples: He drew a deep, mournful note from the violoncello.
She studied the violoncello for ten years before joining the orchestra.

meanings.definition: A vein or deposit of metal ore within rock.
examples: The miners struck a rich lode of silver deep in the hills.
Prospectors searched the canyon for a hidden lode of gold.

meanings.definition: Government or rule by many people.
examples: The council operated as a kind of polycracy, with power shared widely.
Critics warned that the polycracy would lead to endless disagreement.

meanings.definition: To formally charge someone with a crime.
examples: A grand jury voted to indict the senator on bribery charges.
Prosecutors moved to indict the gang's leaders.

meanings.definition: To begin or continue an action, especially after a pause.
examples: After a short break, the meeting proceeded as planned.
Please proceed to the next station when you are ready.

meanings.definition: To move forward or onward.
examples: The procession proceeded slowly down the main avenue.

meanings.definition: A person who holds religious beliefs other than those of the main world religions.
examples: The early missionaries sought to convert the pagans of the region.
To the medieval church, anyone outside the faith was a pagan.

meanings.definition: Relating to such beliefs or their practices.
examples: The festival had its roots in ancient pagan rituals.

meanings.definition: Using pompous, lofty, or inflated language.
examples: His grandiloquent speech impressed no one in the practical-minded crowd.
She mocked the grandiloquent style of the official announcement.

meanings.definition: Affected with rabies.
examples: The rabid dog had to be put down for everyone's safety.
A rabid fox wandered into the village in broad daylight.

meanings.definition: Having or expressing extreme, fanatical enthusiasm or feeling.
examples: He is a rabid supporter of the local football team.

meanings.definition: The act of forcing someone or something out.
examples: His repeated misconduct led to expulsion from the school.
The treaty called for the expulsion of all foreign troops.

meanings.definition: To put someone back into a position or office.
examples: The board voted to reseat the chairman after the inquiry cleared him.
Voters chose to reseat the incumbent for another term.

meanings.definition: To seat someone again or in a different place.
examples: The usher had to reseat the latecomers near the back.

meanings.definition: A smooth, white or lightly tinted fine-grained mineral used for carving.
examples: The vase was carved from a single block of alabaster.
Her skin was as pale and smooth as alabaster.

meanings.definition: Relating to the earliest times or stages of development.
examples: The museum displayed tools used by primitive peoples.
Primitive life forms first appeared in the oceans.

meanings.definition: Simple, crude, or undeveloped.
examples: They cooked over a primitive stove made of stones.

meanings.definition: In the process of happening; being planned or under way.
examples: Rumors suggested that a major change was afoot.
The detective sensed that something sinister was afoot.

meanings.definition: To be about to happen; to loom near.
examples: Dark clouds warned that a storm was about to impend.
A sense of crisis seemed to impend over the whole nation.

meanings.definition: Impossible to explain or account for.
examples: For some inexplicable reason, the lights kept flickering all night.
Her sudden change of heart was completely inexplicable.

meanings.definition: Caused by some force beyond the laws of nature, often by divine or miraculous power.
examples: The old house was said to be home to supernatural beings.
They attributed the healing to supernatural intervention.

meanings.definition: That which lies beyond the natural world.
examples: She had always been fascinated by the supernatural.

meanings.definition: A thing or person that comes before another in time, order, or causality.
examples: The economic crisis was the antecedent of widespread reform.
Historians traced the antecedents of the modern parliament.

meanings.definition: Coming before in time or order; prior.
examples: The antecedent events made the conflict almost inevitable.

meanings.definition: A likeness or similarity in form, quality, or appearance.
examples: There is a strong resemblance between the two sisters.
The painting bears little resemblance to the actual landscape.

meanings.definition: Full of courage, spirit, and energy.
examples: The mettlesome young horse refused to stand still.
She gave a mettlesome reply to her critics.

meanings.definition: Able to be assigned or attributed to a particular cause or source.
examples: The damage was referable to years of neglect.
His symptoms were referable to a vitamin deficiency.

meanings.definition: The dead body of an animal.
examples: Vultures circled the carcass on the desert road.
The butcher hung the carcass in the cold room.

meanings.definition: To regard with blame or disfavor.
examples: Her parents disapprove of staying out so late.
The committee disapproved the proposed budget.

meanings.definition: Directed to one side; sideways.
examples: She gave him a sidelong glance across the room.
He cast a sidelong look at the stranger.

meanings.definition: Having a sharp, strong taste or smell.
examples: The pungent aroma of garlic filled the kitchen.
A pungent smell of smoke drifted through the house.

meanings.definition: Sharply biting or pointed in expression.
examples: The critic was known for his pungent wit.

meanings.definition: To hand over or entrust something to another's care.
examples: She consigned her jewelry to a trusted friend for safekeeping.
The goods were consigned to a warehouse for storage.

meanings.definition: To assign or relegate something to a particular, often undesirable, place or state.
examples: The old files were consigned to the basement and forgotten.

meanings.definition: One who gives or offers a pledge, especially as security for a debt.
examples: As the pledgeor, he handed over the deed as collateral.
The pledgeor retained ownership until the loan was repaid.

meanings.definition: To rebuke or scold someone formally for a fault.
examples: The officer was reprimanded for ignoring protocol.
She reprimanded the students for arriving late.

meanings.definition: To give special importance or stress to something.
examples: The teacher emphasized the need for careful proofreading.
He emphasized each word to make his point clear.

meanings.definition: To wear away or scrape off a surface by friction.
examples: The rough rope abraded the skin on his palms.
Years of wind had abraded the stone carvings.

meanings.definition: One who proves or shows something in a convincing, conclusive way.
examples: The demonstrator showed the audience how the new device worked.
As a skilled demonstrator, she made the complex proof seem simple.

meanings.definition: A person who takes part in a public protest.
examples: Thousands of demonstrators gathered outside the parliament.

meanings.definition: A fixed sum of money paid at regular intervals as a salary or allowance.
examples: The graduate student received a modest monthly stipend.
Her stipend barely covered rent and food.

meanings.definition: Tending to cause or marked by delay; slow to act.
examples: His dilatory tactics held up the negotiations for weeks.
The dilatory response from the office frustrated everyone.

meanings.definition: A person's strong point or area of special skill.
examples: Public speaking has never been my forte.
Cooking is definitely her forte.

meanings.definition: To go without or give up something, especially something desirable.
examples: She decided to forgo dessert to save room for coffee.
They chose to forgo a vacation this year to pay off debts.

meanings.definition: An estimate or conclusion formed without sufficient information.
examples: My guess is that it will rain before noon.
Her answer was nothing more than a lucky guess.

meanings.definition: To form an opinion or estimate without certain knowledge.
examples: Can you guess how many marbles are in the jar?

meanings.definition: A senior naval officer who commands a flagship or is entitled to fly a distinguishing flag.
examples: The flag-officer directed the fleet from the bridge of his flagship.
As a flag-officer, he outranked every captain in the squadron.

meanings.definition: Located farthest to the rear; last.
examples: The hindmost runner finally crossed the finish line.
The hindmost carriages of the train were nearly empty.

meanings.definition: Lack of experience or skill.
examples: His inexperience showed during the first week on the job.
Despite her inexperience, she handled the crisis calmly.

meanings.definition: A feeling of estrangement or separation from others.
examples: Long hours of remote work led to a deep sense of alienation.
His harsh words caused the alienation of his closest friends.

meanings.definition: Not harmful or offensive; harmless.
examples: The snake turned out to be an innocuous garden variety.
It was an innocuous remark that somehow started an argument.

meanings.definition: The quality of being loud and clamorous in speech.
examples: The vociferance of the crowd drowned out the speaker.
His vociferance left no doubt about his anger.

meanings.definition: Dishes and other household earthenware made from baked clay.
examples: She stacked the crockery neatly in the cupboard.
Several pieces of crockery broke when the shelf collapsed.

meanings.definition: To be greater than something else in importance, value, or weight.
examples: The benefits of the plan far outweigh its costs.
Her enthusiasm outweighed her lack of experience.

meanings.definition: Too extreme or unusual to be described in words.
examples: The view from the summit was indescribable.
She felt an indescribable joy when the news arrived.

meanings.definition: A perfect model or example of a particular quality.
examples: She was regarded as a paragon of honesty.
The new building is a paragon of modern design.

meanings.definition: A person in charge of a museum, library, or collection.
examples: The curator arranged the paintings for the new exhibition.
As curator, she selected which artifacts to display.

meanings.definition: The act of fraudulently making or imitating a document, signature, or artwork; something so produced.
examples: He was arrested for forgery after copying the signature.
Experts confirmed the painting was a clever forgery.

meanings.definition: Ordinary and lacking interest or excitement; commonplace.
examples: She longed to escape her mundane daily routine.
Most of the meeting covered mundane administrative details.

meanings.definition: Relating to the everyday world rather than the spiritual or celestial.
examples: The monk withdrew from all mundane concerns.

meanings.definition: To waver indecisively between different opinions or actions.
examples: He vacillated for weeks before accepting the offer.
She vacillated between staying home and going out.

meanings.definition: To block or hinder passage, progress, or action.
examples: A fallen tree obstructed the road for hours.
They were accused of obstructing the investigation.

meanings.definition: Keenness of understanding and insight; shrewdness.
examples: Her perspicacity allowed her to spot the flaw at once.
The detective solved the case through sheer perspicacity.

meanings.definition: To move backward or decline to a worse condition.
examples: Without practice, his skills began to retrograde.
Some feared the reforms would cause society to retrograde.

meanings.definition: Directed or moving backward.
examples: The planet appeared to follow a retrograde path across the sky.

meanings.definition: Consisting of or referring to more than one.
examples: The word 'children' is the plural form of 'child'.
They lived in a plural society of many cultures.

meanings.definition: A grammatical form referring to more than one person or thing.
examples: The plural of 'mouse' is 'mice'.

meanings.definition: A hard, stone-like concretion formed within the body, such as in the kidney.
examples: The surgeon removed a calculus from the patient's kidney.
A calculus had formed in the bladder, causing severe pain.

meanings.definition: The branch of mathematics dealing with rates of change and accumulation.
examples: She struggled with calculus until a tutor explained derivatives.

meanings.definition: A picture or photograph whose edges shade off gradually into the surrounding background.
examples: The portrait was printed as a soft vignette.
Each chapter opened with a small decorative vignette.

meanings.definition: A brief, evocative description or short scene.
examples: The novel is a series of vignettes from village life.

meanings.definition: The whole of a complex system or the universe, regarded as a single entity.
examples: A single cell can mirror the macrocosm of life itself.
The novel uses one family as a macrocosm of the nation.

meanings.definition: A person who occupies or rents land or property from a landlord.
examples: The tenant paid rent on the first of every month.
Several tenants complained about the broken heating.

meanings.definition: The art of painting on freshly applied plaster, especially on walls and ceilings; a painting made this way.
examples: The chapel ceiling is covered with a magnificent fresco.
Michelangelo spent years painting frescoes in Rome.

meanings.definition: To cover or adorn with ornaments.
examples: They bedecked the hall with flowers for the wedding.
The tree was bedecked with twinkling lights.

meanings.definition: Keen insight or discernment.
examples: She analyzed the problem with remarkable penetration.
His penetration into human motives made him a fine novelist.

meanings.definition: The act of passing into or through something.
examples: The penetration of light through the canopy was minimal.

meanings.definition: A group or chain of islands, or a sea containing many islands.
examples: The boat sailed slowly through the archipelago.
Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago.

meanings.definition: To officially give authority, recognition, or credit to someone or something.
examples: The university is accredited by a national board.
The ambassador was accredited to the court in Paris.

meanings.definition: To plan and direct a course, especially of a ship, aircraft, or vehicle.
examples: The captain navigated the ship through the narrow strait.
She navigated the car carefully along the icy road.

meanings.definition: To find one's way through a complex situation or system.
examples: He learned to navigate the company's bureaucracy.

meanings.definition: Utterly wicked or hatefully evil.
examples: The court condemned the heinous crime.
Such heinous acts shocked the entire community.

meanings.definition: Giving or serving as an answer; responsive.
examples: She offered a respondent nod to his question.
The chorus sang a respondent verse to the leader's call.

meanings.definition: A person who replies, especially to a survey or in a legal case.
examples: Most respondents to the survey favored the change.

meanings.definition: Feeling or showing deep dejection from loss of hope.
examples: He grew despondent after losing his job.
She felt despondent when the results came back.

meanings.definition: Carefully choosing only the best or most suitable.
examples: She is very selective about the friends she keeps.
The school has a highly selective admissions process.

meanings.definition: Equivalent in value, effect, or meaning to something else.
examples: His silence was tantamount to an admission of guilt.
Refusing to vote is tantamount to supporting the decision.

meanings.definition: Unexpected and inconvenient or unfortunate.
examples: Barring any untoward events, we should arrive on time.
There was nothing untoward about their meeting.

meanings.definition: A feeling of offense or resentment at a perceived slight.
examples: He took umbrage at the suggestion that he had lied.
She felt some umbrage at being left off the list.

meanings.definition: A non-commissioned military or police officer ranking above a corporal.
examples: The sergeant ordered the recruits to fall in line.
A police sergeant took charge of the investigation.

meanings.definition: So plentiful that it can never be used up; limitless.
examples: She seemed to have an inexhaustible supply of energy.
The library was an inexhaustible source of stories.

meanings.definition: The attainment of a position of rank, dignity, or office.
examples: The queen's accession to the throne was celebrated nationwide.
His accession to the presidency marked a new era.

meanings.definition: A new item added to a collection.
examples: The museum's latest accession was a rare medieval manuscript.

meanings.definition: Done in a secret and dishonest way.
examples: He won the contract through underhanded tactics.
Their underhanded scheme was eventually exposed.

meanings.definition: Relating to St. Augustine, his teachings, or the religious orders named after him.
examples: The monastery followed the Augustinian rule.
Her thesis examined Augustinian views on grace.

meanings.definition: To move about in a busy, hurried, or energetic way.
examples: Shoppers bustled through the crowded market.
She bustled around the kitchen preparing dinner.

meanings.definition: Having the nature or form of a question.
examples: He gave her an interrogative look.
The sentence ended with an interrogative tone.

meanings.definition: A word used in asking a question, such as 'who' or 'why'.
examples: 'When' is an interrogative used to ask about time.

meanings.definition: An estate of land held under the feudal system in return for service.
examples: The knight was granted a fief by the king.
He ruled his fief with an iron hand.

meanings.definition: To put someone or something at risk of harm.
examples: Pollution endangers many species of fish.
Driving recklessly endangers everyone on the road.

meanings.definition: A movement of the hands, face, or body expressing an idea or feeling.
examples: She waved her hand in a friendly gesture.
His gestures became more animated as he spoke.

meanings.definition: An action done to convey a feeling or intention, often symbolically.
examples: The flowers were a gesture of apology.

meanings.definition: To get down from a horse, bicycle, or other mount.
examples: The rider dismounted and led the horse to the stable.
She dismounted from her bicycle at the gate.

meanings.definition: To remove something from its support or mounting.
examples: They dismounted the cannon from the wall.

meanings.definition: To declare or affirm something as true of a subject.
examples: Philosophers predicate goodness of certain actions.
One cannot predicate intelligence of a machine.

meanings.definition: To base or found something on a particular fact or condition.
examples: The plan was predicated on continued economic growth.

meanings.definition: An intense passion for collecting and owning books.
examples: His bibliomania filled every room with towering shelves.
Her bibliomania led her to bid heavily at rare-book auctions.

meanings.definition: Quick and light in movement or action.
examples: The nimble dancer leaped across the stage.
Her nimble fingers flew over the piano keys.

meanings.definition: Quick to understand or respond; mentally sharp.
examples: His nimble mind grasped the puzzle at once.

meanings.definition: To kiss.
examples: In old poetry, lovers would osculate beneath the moonlight.
The couple osculated tenderly before parting at the station.

meanings.definition: In mathematics, to touch a curve or surface so as to have contact of a higher order than tangency.
examples: The two circles osculate at a single shared point.

meanings.definition: The presence of something in only small amounts; scarcity or fewness.
examples: The paucity of evidence made it impossible to convict the suspect.
A paucity of rain that spring left the reservoirs dangerously low.

meanings.definition: Having a strong, pleasant flavor that affects the sense of taste.
examples: The chef prized sapid herbs that woke up an otherwise dull broth.
A sapid wine lingered agreeably on the palate.

meanings.definition: Engaging or lively to the mind.
examples: Her sapid conversation kept the dinner guests entertained for hours.

meanings.definition: Happening or operating at the same time.
examples: He served two concurrent prison sentences.
The festival ran concurrent with the city's anniversary celebrations.

meanings.definition: Acting together or in agreement.
examples: The judges were concurrent in their final verdict.

meanings.definition: A strong, sweet alcoholic drink flavored with fruits, herbs, or other aromatic substances.
examples: She poured a coffee liqueur over the vanilla ice cream.
After dinner they sipped a herbal liqueur from tiny glasses.

meanings.definition: A blessing or expression of good wishes.
examples: The old priest gave the travelers his benison before they set out.
She regarded the gentle rain as a benison after the long drought.

meanings.definition: A large military unit consisting of two or more regiments or battalions.
examples: The brigade advanced at dawn toward the enemy lines.
An infantry brigade was deployed to hold the bridge.

meanings.definition: A group of people organized for a particular purpose or activity.
examples: The fire brigade arrived within minutes of the alarm.

meanings.definition: To organize people into a group or unit for a purpose.
examples: Volunteers were quickly brigaded to fill sandbags along the river.

meanings.definition: To bring together or combine into a single whole.
examples: The leader sought to unify the divided factions under one banner.
A common language helped unify the scattered tribes.

meanings.definition: Capable of being extended, stretched out, or enlarged.
examples: The snail's extensible tentacles probed the leaf for food.
The software is extensible, allowing developers to add new features.

meanings.definition: Relating to a town or city or its local government.
examples: The municipal council voted to repave the downtown streets.
Municipal taxes fund local schools and parks.

meanings.definition: A person who presides over a meeting, debate, or discussion.
examples: The moderator kept the candidates to their allotted speaking time.
As moderator, she ensured every member had a chance to be heard.

meanings.definition: A person who reviews and oversees content or behavior in an online community.
examples: A moderator removed the offensive comment from the forum.

meanings.definition: Lacking in honor, character, or moral worth.
examples: He was driven by the ignoble desire for revenge.
It would be ignoble to abandon a friend in distress.

meanings.definition: Amusing or laughable, especially in an odd or unexpected way.
examples: The puppy's comical attempt to climb the stairs made everyone laugh.
He told the story with such comical timing that the room erupted.

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of experience, refinement, or worldly knowledge.
examples: Her unsophisticated charm endeared her to the city crowd.
The unsophisticated equipment could not handle the complex task.

meanings.definition: Awareness or knowledge of something before it happens.
examples: The captain had no foreknowledge of the coming storm.
With foreknowledge of the layoffs, she began searching for a new job.

meanings.definition: An ornament or object that hangs from a chain, necklace, or other support.
examples: She wore a silver pendant shaped like a star.
A crystal pendant dangled from the rear-view mirror.

meanings.definition: The period during which an office, position, or property is held.
examples: During her tenure as mayor, the city built three new libraries.
His tenure on the board lasted nearly two decades.

meanings.definition: A permanent post, especially a guaranteed academic position.
examples: After years of research, the professor was finally granted tenure.

meanings.definition: Situated on the far side of the Alps, especially from the perspective of Italy.
examples: The Romans launched campaigns into transalpine Gaul.
Transalpine trade routes carried goods over the mountain passes.

meanings.definition: So bad or shocking that it cannot be expressed in words.
examples: The survivors had endured unspeakable horrors during the war.
He felt an unspeakable grief at the loss of his child.

meanings.definition: Too great or intense to describe.
examples: She felt unspeakable joy when she saw her family waiting at the gate.

meanings.definition: To repeatedly trouble, pester, or annoy someone.
examples: Reporters continued to harass the actor outside his home.
Stop harassing your sister with those endless questions.

meanings.definition: To make repeated small-scale attacks on an enemy.
examples: Guerrilla fighters harassed the convoy throughout the night.

meanings.definition: A person who works to improve or correct something, especially a system or institution.
examples: The social reformer campaigned tirelessly for child labor laws.
As a reformer, she sought to root out corruption in the department.

meanings.definition: A smooth, greasy preparation applied to the skin for healing or cosmetic purposes.
examples: The nurse rubbed a soothing ointment onto the burn.
He kept a tube of antiseptic ointment in the first-aid kit.

meanings.definition: A member of the nobility or a person of high social rank by birth.
examples: The aristocrat owned a vast estate in the countryside.
She carried herself with the cool poise of a born aristocrat.

meanings.definition: Showing bravery in the face of danger or difficulty.
examples: It was courageous of her to speak out against the powerful company.
The courageous firefighter ran back into the burning building.

meanings.definition: A gesture or offer of peace or reconciliation.
examples: After years of feuding, he extended an olive branch to his brother.
The new policy was seen as an olive branch to the opposition.

meanings.definition: A flat, usually rectangular section set into a surface such as a door, wall, or frame.
examples: The carpenter replaced a cracked panel in the cabinet door.
Sunlight glinted off the solar panel on the roof.

meanings.definition: A group of people gathered to discuss, judge, or decide something.
examples: A panel of experts reviewed the research findings.

meanings.definition: Requiring immediate action or attention; urgent.
examples: The exigent circumstances forced the crew to abandon ship.
Police entered without a warrant under exigent conditions.

meanings.definition: Demanding more than is reasonable; exacting.
examples: Her exigent employer expected reports finished overnight.

meanings.definition: A group of individuals or organizations combined to carry out a particular enterprise.
examples: A syndicate of investors bought the struggling football club.
The lottery was won by a syndicate of office workers.

meanings.definition: An association that sells articles or features to multiple newspapers or magazines.
examples: Her comic strip was picked up by a national syndicate.

meanings.definition: Relating to hell or resembling it; fiendish.
examples: The novel described the infernal torments awaiting the damned.
An infernal glow flickered from the depths of the volcano.

meanings.definition: Extremely annoying or detestable.
examples: Turn off that infernal racket so I can think!

meanings.definition: A design made by combining two or more letters, usually a person's initials.
examples: His handkerchiefs were embroidered with a gold monogram.
The luggage bore her elegant monogram in the corner.

meanings.definition: To decorate an item with a monogram.
examples: She had the towels monogrammed as a wedding gift.

meanings.definition: Extreme fierceness, savagery, or intensity.
examples: The storm struck the coast with unexpected ferocity.
He defended his title with the ferocity of a cornered animal.

meanings.definition: Relating to fireworks or their manufacture and display.
examples: The festival ended with a dazzling pyrotechnic show over the harbor.
Strict laws govern the sale of pyrotechnic materials.

meanings.definition: Brilliantly or spectacularly impressive.
examples: The pianist gave a pyrotechnic performance that stunned the audience.

meanings.definition: In Greek mythology, the goddess of retribution who punished pride and wrongdoing.
examples: The arrogant king feared the coming of Nemesis.
Ancient Greeks invoked Nemesis to restore balance after excess.

meanings.definition: A long-standing rival or an unavoidable agent of someone's downfall.
examples: The detective finally cornered his lifelong nemesis.

meanings.definition: Bitterness or harshness of speech, temper, or manner.
examples: The negotiations collapsed amid mutual acrimony.
She spoke of her former partner without a trace of acrimony.

meanings.definition: Relating to the throat or neck.
examples: The doctor checked the swelling near the jugular region.
Pressure on the jugular vein can cause dizziness.

meanings.definition: One of the large veins in the neck carrying blood from the head.
examples: The predator instinctively went for the prey's jugular.

meanings.definition: To drive, push, or move something forward.
examples: Powerful engines propel the rocket into orbit.
A strong tailwind propelled the sailboat across the bay.

meanings.definition: To urge or motivate someone toward a goal.
examples: Ambition propelled her to the top of her profession.

meanings.definition: A standard or principle used to judge or decide something.
examples: Affordability was the main criterion in choosing a new home.
Each applicant was assessed against the same criterion.

meanings.definition: The study of the fundamental nature of knowledge, reality, and existence.
examples: She studied ancient Greek philosophy at university.
Questions of free will lie at the heart of philosophy.

meanings.definition: A particular set of beliefs or guiding principles.
examples: His business philosophy puts customers above short-term profit.

meanings.definition: Operated by or relating to air or gas under pressure.
examples: The mechanic loosened the bolts with a pneumatic drill.
Pneumatic tubes once carried mail across the building.

meanings.definition: A vocal composition with instrumental accompaniment, typically with several movements and a choir.
examples: The choir performed a Bach cantata at the Christmas service.
The composer wrote a cantata to celebrate the city's founding.

meanings.definition: An object that produces a magnetic field and attracts iron or steel.
examples: She stuck the photo to the fridge with a colorful magnet.
The horseshoe magnet picked up the scattered nails.

meanings.definition: A person or place that attracts people or things strongly.
examples: The new theme park became a magnet for tourists.

meanings.definition: To behave as if one is superior while dealing with others.
examples: He tends to condescend to anyone without a college degree.
Please don't condescend to me; I understand the situation.

meanings.definition: To lower oneself to do something one considers beneath one's dignity.
examples: The famous author finally condescended to sign a few autographs.

meanings.definition: An earnest or urgent appeal for something.
examples: The charity issued a desperate plea for donations.
Her plea for mercy moved the entire courtroom.

meanings.definition: A defendant's formal statement of guilt or innocence in a court of law.
examples: The accused entered a plea of not guilty.

meanings.definition: The act of killing one's own parent or other close relative.
examples: The ancient tragedy turns on the crime of parricide.
He was charged with parricide after his father's death.

meanings.definition: A person who kills their own parent.
examples: In legend, the parricide was pursued relentlessly by the Furies.

meanings.definition: Relating to worldly or earthly affairs rather than spiritual ones.
examples: The church held both spiritual and temporal power in the region.
He set aside temporal concerns to focus on his faith.

meanings.definition: Relating to or limited by time.
examples: The film plays with temporal order, jumping between past and present.

meanings.definition: A fixed sum of money paid to someone each year, typically for the rest of their life.
examples: She bought an annuity to provide steady income in retirement.
The pension was paid out as a monthly annuity.

meanings.definition: Effectively conveying thought, emotion, or meaning.
examples: The dancer's expressive movements told the whole story.
She has wonderfully expressive eyes.

meanings.definition: A steep, rugged rock projecting from a cliff or mountainside.
examples: The climbers inched along a narrow ledge below the crag.
An eagle nested on the windswept crag above the valley.

meanings.definition: The act of placing or coming between two things or parties.
examples: The interposition of a screen blocked the harsh light.
Peace was restored through the timely interposition of a mediator.

meanings.definition: To make someone or something beloved or dear to another.
examples: Her kindness endeared her to the whole neighborhood.
His honesty endeared him to voters across the country.

meanings.definition: The detention of a ship, freight car, or other vessel beyond its scheduled departure or loading time.
examples: The delay at the port caused several days of demurrage.
Heavy demurrage charges piled up while the cargo sat unloaded.

meanings.definition: A charge payable for such delay.
examples: The shipper had to pay demurrage on the late container.

meanings.definition: To give someone greater knowledge or understanding about something.
examples: The lecture enlightened us about the causes of the war.
Perhaps you can enlighten me on how this machine works.

meanings.definition: The origin or coming into being of something.
examples: The genesis of the company can be traced to a small garage.
We discussed the genesis of her latest novel.

meanings.definition: To scatter, disperse, or gradually disappear.
examples: The morning fog dissipated as the sun rose higher.
Her fears dissipated once she heard the good news.

meanings.definition: To waste or squander money, energy, or resources.
examples: He dissipated his inheritance on lavish parties.

meanings.definition: Relating to the stars.
examples: The telescope captured stunning stellar formations.
Astronomers measured the stellar distances with great precision.

meanings.definition: Outstanding or exceptionally good.
examples: She gave a stellar performance on opening night.

meanings.definition: A professional who calculates risks and premiums, especially for an insurance company.
examples: The actuary determined the premiums for the new life-insurance policy.
As an actuary, she analyzed mortality data all day.

meanings.definition: A person who supports reorganizing society through collective or public ownership of land and capital.
examples: The socialist argued that essential services should be publicly owned.
As a committed socialist, she championed workers' rights.

meanings.definition: Relating to or advocating socialism.
examples: The party adopted a socialist platform calling for nationalized industry.

meanings.definition: To press or squeeze something into a smaller space.
examples: The machine compresses the cardboard into tight bales.
You can compress the files to save storage space.

meanings.definition: To condense or shorten something, such as time or text.
examples: The editor compressed the long report into a single page.

meanings.definition: To make air, water, or land dirty or harmful with waste or contaminants.
examples: The factory was fined for polluting the river.
Exhaust fumes pollute the air in crowded cities.

meanings.definition: To corrupt or spoil something.
examples: Such rumors only pollute the public's trust in science.

meanings.definition: An adult male horse that has not been castrated, often kept for breeding.
examples: The black stallion galloped across the open field.
The ranch is famous for its champion breeding stallion.

meanings.definition: A large musical work for orchestra, choir, and soloists, usually on a religious theme.
examples: Handel's Messiah is the most famous oratorio ever written.
The choir spent months rehearsing the lengthy oratorio.

meanings.definition: Lively, spirited, or exhilarating.
examples: The novel moved along at a racy, fast-paced clip.
She drove a racy little sports car.

meanings.definition: Mildly sexually suggestive or risqué.
examples: The comedian told a few racy jokes that made the audience blush.

meanings.definition: A state of prolonged tiredness, sluggishness, or lack of energy.
examples: A heavy lunch left him in a state of lethargy all afternoon.
The illness brought on a deep lethargy she struggled to shake.

meanings.definition: Breaking out or becoming active again after a period of dormancy.
examples: Doctors monitored the recrudescent fever closely.
A recrudescent outbreak of the disease alarmed the village.

meanings.definition: To officially cancel or withdraw something previously granted.
examples: The court revoked his driving license after the offense.
The company revoked the offer when the references failed to check out.

meanings.definition: A person who makes or conveys a grant, especially the one transferring property in a deed.
examples: The grantor signed the deed transferring the land to the buyer.
As grantor, she retained the right to live in the house for life.

meanings.definition: Existing in or having many different shapes, forms, or kinds.
examples: The festival offered a multiform array of music and dance.
Nature reveals itself in multiform beauty across the seasons.

meanings.definition: An annual publication giving a calendar with astronomical data and other useful information.
examples: The farmer consulted his almanac to plan the planting season.
According to the almanac, the next full moon falls on Friday.

meanings.definition: A person who handles correspondence, records, and other clerical work for an individual or organization.
examples: The secretary scheduled all of the director's meetings.
She worked as a legal secretary for a downtown firm.

meanings.definition: A senior official heading a government department.
examples: The Secretary of State addressed the United Nations.

meanings.definition: The quality of being important or worthy of attention.
examples: Historians still debate the significance of the treaty.
She didn't grasp the significance of the discovery at first.

meanings.definition: The meaning conveyed by something.
examples: The significance of the symbol was lost on most viewers.

meanings.definition: To smear or coat all over with something oily, sticky, or gaudy.
examples: The children bedaubed the fence with thick, dripping paint.
His coat was bedaubed with mud after the long ride.

meanings.definition: A coil, twist, or winding turn of something.
examples: The convolution of the river made the journey twice as long.
We traced the convolutions of the snail's shell.

meanings.definition: An intricate, complicated form or part of something.
examples: The convolutions of the legal argument confused even the lawyers.

meanings.definition: Relating to the philosophical study of the fundamental nature of reality and existence.
examples: The lecture raised metaphysical questions about the nature of time.
She enjoyed metaphysical debates about free will.

meanings.definition: Beyond what is physical or concrete; abstract or supernatural.
examples: He spoke of a metaphysical connection between the twins.

meanings.definition: The status, office, or dignity of being a king; royal authority.
examples: He inherited the kingship after his father's death.
The kingship carried both great power and great responsibility.

meanings.definition: Having or arousing an excessive interest in sexual matters.
examples: The tabloid catered to its readers' prurient curiosity.
Critics condemned the film's prurient depiction of the scandal.

meanings.definition: The final part of a play or story in which the plot is resolved and mysteries are explained.
examples: The novel's denouement revealed who had committed the crime.
Everything fell into place during the satisfying denouement.

meanings.definition: A word or phrase used in ordinary, informal conversation rather than formal speech.
examples: "Gonna" is a common colloquialism for "going to."
The essay was marred by too many slang colloquialisms.

meanings.definition: Corresponding in size or amount to something else; in proportion.
examples: The punishment should be proportionate to the crime.
Each worker received a bonus proportionate to their hours.

meanings.definition: A distinct incident or event that forms part of a larger story or sequence.
examples: That embarrassing episode still makes him cringe.
The biography devotes a chapter to each episode of her career.

meanings.definition: A single installment of a serialized television or radio program.
examples: We watched the final episode of the season last night.

meanings.definition: A strong fortress, especially one protecting or dominating a city.
examples: The ancient citadel still towers over the old town.
Soldiers retreated to the citadel to make their last stand.

meanings.definition: Devoutly religious; showing reverence for God.
examples: She was a pious woman who prayed every morning.
His pious upbringing shaped his strict moral code.

meanings.definition: Making a hypocritical or insincere show of virtue.
examples: I'm tired of his pious lectures about honesty.

meanings.definition: To imitate or reproduce the appearance, character, or conditions of something.
examples: The flight program can simulate a storm for trainee pilots.
She tried to simulate enthusiasm she did not feel.

meanings.definition: The practice of inducing a trance-like state in which a person is highly responsive to suggestion.
examples: He studied hypnotism to help patients quit smoking.
Through hypnotism the therapist eased her deep-seated fear.

meanings.definition: To light up or make bright; to enlighten.
examples: Lanterns illumined the garden path at dusk.
A single insight can illumine an entire mystery.

meanings.definition: Something said or done on the spur of the moment without preparation.
examples: Her speech was a brilliant impromptu that delighted the crowd.
The musician treated us to a lively impromptu on the piano.

meanings.definition: Done without rehearsal or planning; spontaneous.
examples: They held an impromptu meeting in the hallway.

meanings.definition: Reluctant or unwilling to do something.
examples: She was loath to leave the warm fire.
He seemed loath to admit that he had been wrong.

meanings.definition: Impossible to carry out or put into practice.
examples: The plan proved impracticable given our limited budget.
Crossing the flooded road was impracticable that night.

meanings.definition: To make someone intensely angry.
examples: The unfair ruling enraged the entire crowd.
Nothing enraged him more than being ignored.

meanings.definition: A substance or source that gives off or produces light.
examples: In the past, whale oil was a common illuminant.
Engineers tested a new illuminant for street lamps.

meanings.definition: To choose not to do or take part in something, especially by self-restraint.
examples: He decided to abstain from alcohol for a month.
Three council members chose to abstain from the vote.

meanings.definition: Relating to trade, commerce, or the conduct of business.
examples: The city grew rich through its mercantile activities.
His family had a long mercantile tradition of shipping goods.

meanings.definition: To sharpen the edge of a blade or tool.
examples: He whet the knife on a stone before carving the roast.

meanings.definition: To stimulate or make more eager, especially appetite or interest.
examples: The aroma whetted our appetite for dinner.
The trailer only whetted my curiosity about the film.

meanings.definition: To make the convulsive motions of vomiting, especially without bringing anything up.
examples: The foul smell made him retch.
She began to retch at the sight of the wound.

meanings.definition: A public written declaration of the intentions, motives, or views of a person or group.
examples: The party published its manifesto before the election.
The artist's manifesto called for a break with tradition.

meanings.definition: A person one works with, especially in a profession.
examples: She asked a colleague to cover her shift.
My colleagues and I are presenting at the conference.

meanings.definition: A small statue or figurine.
examples: A bronze statuette of a dancer stood on the mantel.
She won a gold statuette at the awards ceremony.

meanings.definition: A group of families or people descended from a common ancestor; a tribe.
examples: The Highland clans gathered for the annual games.
Rival clans had feuded for generations.

meanings.definition: A large, close-knit group of relatives or associates.
examples: The whole clan shows up for Sunday dinner.

meanings.definition: The state of having power, influence, or control over others.
examples: The team's dominance over its rivals was undeniable.
She established her dominance in the field early on.

meanings.definition: A word consisting of two syllables.
examples: "Table" is a disyllable, while "cat" has only one syllable.
The poet favored crisp disyllables over longer words.

meanings.definition: More than is needed, useful, or wanted; excessive.
examples: The editor cut every superfluous word from the essay.
Adding more proof now would be superfluous.
